preFix.title: Understanding clostridial diseases and vaccination
preFix.subtitle: The group of diseases prevented by the standard sheep vaccine, and why the timing matters.
preFix.excerpt: The annual clostridial vaccination is the single most cost-effective health intervention in sheep keeping. The eight diseases it covers are, in most cases, fatal within hours of first symptoms. Understanding which diseases the vaccine prevents and when to give it is more useful than recognising the symptoms, because by the time symptoms appear, treatment is rarely possible.
preFix.sourceNotes: AHDB Beef and Lamb, Clostridial Disease factsheet (ahdb.org.uk). SAC Consulting Scotland, Sheep Health Notes. DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Sheep, 2020.

content.text: vaccine (the 7-in-1 or 8-in-1) covers the main bacterial causes of sudden death in sheep. Unvaccinated lambs on spring grass are at high risk of pulpy kidney. Unvaccinated adults risk tetanus after any wound or birthing injury.

Pulpy kidney (enterotoxaemia type D) is the most frequently encountered. It kills fast-growing lambs abruptly, often the best lambs in the flock, when a sudden change to rich pasture causes Clostridium perfringens to proliferate rapidly in the gut. The lamb is found dead with no warning signs. Lamb dysentery (type B) causes bloody diarrhoea and rapid death in lambs under 3 weeks. Tetanus follows contaminated wounds; castration and tailing are the most common routes in young lambs.
The primary course and the booster timing
Unvaccinated adults need a primary course of two injections given 4-6 weeks apart. After the primary course, an annual

maintains immunity. For ewes, the booster timing is the critical decision: give it 4-6 weeks before the expected lambing date. This elevates the ewe's antibody level at exactly the point when her colostrum is being produced, maximising the passive immunity she passes to the lamb.
Lambs: when direct vaccination takes effect

Lambs that received adequate colostrum from a well-vaccinated ewe are protected for the first 8-12 weeks of life by maternal antibodies. After that, the protection wanes. In a flock where lambs are sold before 12 weeks, no further vaccination of lambs is necessary. Lambs kept beyond 12 weeks need their own primary course starting at 8-12 weeks to ensure continuous protection through the fast-growing autumn period when pulpy kidney risk is highest.

Any ewe that joins the flock without vaccination records should be treated as unvaccinated. Give her a primary course straight away, regardless of her paperwork, and schedule her booster 4-6 weeks before her first lambing on your holding.
